I agree with the final selection of D as the correct answer. One key point often overlooked is that while insulin itself doesn’t cross the placenta, switching from oral agents to insulin involves careful monitoring of dosages to avoid hypoglycemic episodes in the newborn, especially those caused by abrupt cessation of maternal sulfonylurea intake. Additionally, there may still be lingering issues with residual hyperinsulinism even after transitioning off tolbutamide, underscoring why discontinuing such agents early in favor of continuous insulin administration is generally advised. These nuances support keeping D as the exceptional case where treatment strategy shifts away from relying purely on oral agents despite no inherent toxicity transfer through placentation.
